summary:
Epicureanism (founded c. 307–270 BC) was a philosophical school established by Epicurus in Athens, which taught that pleasure — specifically ataraxia (tranquility of mind) and aponia (freedom from bodily pain) — is the highest good, that the gods do not intervene in human affairs, and that death is simply the cessation of consciousness and need not be feared — a philosophy that offered comfort in a turbulent Hellenistic world.

Epicurus founded his school in a garden outside Athens (hence followers were called 'Garden' philosophers), welcoming women and slaves as equals in an unusual departure from social norms. His physics drew on Democritus's atomism — the universe consists of atoms in void, with no supernatural dimension — which grounded his ethics: if gods don't interfere and death is mere non-existence, one need not fear punishment or oblivion. Epicurean pleasure was primarily negative (freedom from pain and anxiety) rather than active sensual enjoyment, despite the word 'epicurean' later acquiring connotations of luxurious excess. The philosophy attracted devoted followers across the Hellenistic and Roman worlds, including the poet [[event:1248|Lucretius and De Rerum Natura]]'s Lucretius, whose masterwork De Rerum Natura (On the Nature of Things) preserved Epicurean philosophy in poetic form and proved enormously influential on later Renaissance and Enlightenment materialism.
